summary: Covert remote-controlled hijack of installer's WhatsApp account via encrypted C2 config
details: alya-baileys is a fork of the Baileys WhatsApp library that adds undisclosed remote-control behavior over the installer's connected WhatsApp account. On every connection open, lib/Socket/newsletter.js fetches a JSON config from a URL stored as an AES-256-CBC-encrypted hex blob (key derived from the string 'Alyabaileys', IV 'alyamoonalyamoo'); the decrypted endpoint is hosted on raw.githubusercontent.com and is mutable by the package author. The fetched config dynamically controls a list of newsletter JIDs the installer's WhatsApp account silently auto-follows, auto-reacts to (with random or keyword-keyed emojis), and auto-casts poll votes for, via a 'messages.upsert' handler registered unconditionally inside makeNewsletterSocket. None of this is exposed in the documented API. lib/Utils/alya.js wraps the decryption with deliberately misleading helpers (the file pads in 5-6 decoy `require('./alya')` aliases named hapus_aja / gak_penting_asli / _apacoba_wkwk — Indonesian for 'just delete', 'not important', 'just trying'), and an in-source comment at newsletter.js:196 reads 'You'll never find what this does - Ibra Decode'. The combination of (a) a hidden, author-mutable C2 endpoint concealed by AES encryption, (b) silent use of the installer's authenticated WhatsApp identity to follow/react/vote on author-chosen targets, and (c) gratuitous obfuscation and decoy imports purpose-built to hide the relay from reviewers makes this a silent-relay backdoor over the installer's account.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
